stem: A retail study measures customer satisfaction score across 5 chains, each containing multiple stores. chain A has 10 observations with mean 92.7 /10 (within-chain SD = 7.4). chain B has 8 observations with mean 67.4 /10 (within-chain SD = 8.1). chain C has 20 observations with mean 62.6 /10 (within-chain SD = 8.6). chain D has 20 observations with mean 81.2 /10 (within-chain SD = 8.0). chain E has 8 observations with mean 48.8 /10 (within-chain SD = 5.5). The between-chain standard deviation is uncertain, estimated between 8.7 and 21.8 /10. Using a hierarchical Bayesian model, estimate the true customer satisfaction score for chain D, accounting for shrinkage toward the grand mean and the uncertainty in the between-chain variation.

ground_truth.answer: Hierarchical estimate for chain D: 81.1 /10 (shrunk from raw 81.2). Range [80.8, 81.1] as between-chain SD varies from 8.7 to 21.8. Pooled mean: 71.7.

solution_steps: Compute pooled (grand) mean: 71.7 /10 (weighted average of 5 chain means).
Shrinkage factor for chain D: B = σ²_between / (σ²_between + σ²_within/20). At σ_between=14.5: B = 0.985.
Interval [80.8, 81.1] captures uncertainty from the unknown between-group variance.
common_failure_modes: Using the raw observed mean (81.2) without shrinkage
Using only the grand mean (71.7) and ignoring group-specific data
Not recognizing that small-sample groups should be shrunk more toward the grand mean
Treating the between-group variance as known rather than uncertain
